t: Khao Soi (Northern Thai Coconut Curry Noodles)
blurb: Chiang Mai's iconic curry noodle soup with tender chicken in rich coconut broth, topped with crispy noodles.

prov.famous_for: Signature dish of Chiang Mai; coconut curry broth with both soft and crispy egg noodles
prov.consists_of: Egg noodles in coconut curry broth with chicken, topped with crispy fried noodles and condiments
prov.name_origin: Thai words 'khao' (rice) and 'soi' (to cut/slice); evolved from 'Papa Soi' hand-sliced noodles introduced by Yunnanese Muslim traders
prov.why_loved: Perfect balance of rich, creamy coconut curry with fresh, crunchy condiments and the irresistible contrast of soft and crispy noodles
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — chicken simmered in coconut curry paste broth, served with both boiled and fried noodles

brief.lead: The iconic noodle soup of Northern Thailand, featuring tender chicken in a rich, aromatic coconut curry broth, served with the signature dual texture of soft boiled noodles and crispy fried noodles, finished with a vibrant array of fresh condiments.
expect: Coconut Curry Broth
Rich, creamy, and aromatic with curry paste, turmeric, and coconut milk - the soul of the dish.
Dual Noodle Texture
Soft boiled egg noodles swimming in broth topped with crispy fried noodles for irresistible contrast.
Tender Chicken
Falling-off-the-bone chicken thighs that absorb the curry flavours during simmering.
Fresh Condiments
Crisp bean sprouts, shallots, spring onions, coriander, and pickled mustard greens to cut through the richness.
kit: Wok or deep pan (for frying noodles)

tl: Heat coconut milk, bloom curry paste with aromatics
Add chicken and simmer 40 min until tender
Fry portion of noodles until crispy (T+20)
Compose bowls with broth, noodles, chicken, toppings
steps: Bloom the Curry Paste

In a large pot over medium heat, add the thick cream from the top of the coconut milk (about 3 tablespoons). Add the curry paste, curry powder, and turmeric. Fry for 2-3 minutes, stirring constantly, until the paste is fragrant and starts to separate from the oil - this releases the essential oils and intensifies the flavours.
WHY: Blooming the spices in coconut fat unlocks their aromatic compounds, creating the rich base that defines the dish.
Build the Broth

Add the minced garlic, sliced shallots, galangal, and makrut lime leaves. Fry for 1 minute until fragrant. Pour in the remaining coconut milk and water or stock. Add fish sauce and palm sugar. Bring to a gentle simmer, stirring to dissolve the sugar.
WHY: Building the broth in layers allows each aromatic to infuse properly before adding the chicken.
Simmer the Chicken

Add the chicken leg quarters to the simmering broth. Cover and cook gently for 40 minutes, or until the chicken is tender and falling off the bone. Skim any excess oil that rises to the surface if desired. Adjust seasoning with fish sauce or sugar to taste - it should be rich, savoury, and slightly sweet.
WHY: Gentle simmering renders the collagen in the chicken, creating tender meat and enriching the broth without breaking the coconut milk.
Prepare the Noodles

While the chicken simmers, separate the egg noodles into two portions. Boil one portion (2/3) according to package instructions until just tender - about 2-3 minutes. Drain and set aside. For the remaining portion (1/3), heat oil in a wok or deep pan to 180°C and fry the noodles until crispy and golden - about 1-2 minutes. Drain on paper towels.
WHY: The dual texture of soft and crispy noodles is the signature of khao soi - the boiled noodles absorb the broth while the fried ones add crunch.
Assemble and Serve

Place a portion of boiled noodles in each serving bowl. Ladle the hot broth over the noodles (about 1-1.5 cups per bowl). Top with a piece of chicken. Garnish generously with crispy fried noodles, bean sprouts, spring onions, crispy shallots, pickled mustard greens, and coriander. Serve immediately with lime wedges and chilli oil on the side.
WHY: Assembling table-side allows each diner to customise their bowl with fresh condiments that brighten and cut through the rich curry.
Optional: Chilli Oil
For an authentic touch, make a quick chilli oil by frying dried chilli flakes in oil until fragrant, then drizzle over the finished bowls. This adds both heat and visual appeal.
WHY: Chilli oil provides an extra layer of heat and the vibrant red colour that's characteristic of street-style khao soi.
pair: Crisp and refreshing lager to cut through the rich coconut curry and cleanse the palate between bites.
A small bowl of hot and sour tom yum to awaken the palate before the rich and creamy khao soi.

blurb: Rich and aromatic Thai-Muslim curry with tender beef, potatoes, and peanuts in a creamy coconut sauce with warm spices.

prov.popular_in: Southern Thailand; also popular in Central Thailand and internationally
prov.famous_for: Incorporating Persian/Indian spices like cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves; one of the mildest Thai curries with complex aromatic layers
prov.consists_of: Beef or chicken simmered in coconut milk with potatoes, onions, and roasted peanuts, flavoured with Massaman curry paste and warm spices
prov.name_origin: From 'Muslim', referring to the dish's origins among Thai Muslim traders with Persian influences; evolved from Malay-Indian curry traditions brought to Southern Thailand
prov.why_loved: Gentle, approachable heat with deep, complex spices - cardamom's floral notes, cinnamon's warmth, and peanuts' nuttiness create sophisticated comfort food that appeals to all palates
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — meat and vegetables slowly simmered in spiced coconut broth until tender and infused

brief.lead: A rich and aromatic curry that bridges Thai and Muslim culinary traditions, featuring tender beef chuck slowly simmered with potatoes in a luxurious coconut broth infused with warm Persian spices - cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves - and finished with roasted peanuts for a nutty, sophisticated finish.
expect: Complex Spice Profile
Cardamom's floral notes, cinnamon's warmth, and cloves' depth create layers of aromatic sophistication beyond typical Thai curries.
Tender Beef
Chuck beef slowly melts into succulence during the 2-hour simmer, absorbing the spiced coconut broth.
Creamy Coconut Base
Rich coconut milk tempered with spices creates a luxurious, velvety sauce that coats every ingredient.
Nutty Finish
Roasted peanuts add a delightful crunch and nutty depth that balances the sweet and savoury elements.

ing: beef chuck, cut into 4 cm chunks
For the Spice Infusion (optional but authentic)
tl: Prep ingredients; bloom curry paste in coconut cream
Brown beef chunks in batches; add spices
Add liquids; simmer beef 90 min until tender
Add potatoes and onions; cook 30 min more
Add peanuts, adjust seasoning, garnish, serve
steps: Bloom the Curry Paste

In a large heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat, scoop out the thick cream from the top of the coconut milk (about 3 tablespoons). Add the Massaman curry paste and fry for 2-3 minutes, stirring constantly, until the paste is fragrant and the oil starts to separate - this 'blooming' releases the paste's aromatic compounds.
WHY: Cooking the paste in coconut fat intensifies the flavours and creates a rich base for the curry.
Sear the Beef

Increase the heat to medium-high. Add the beef chunks in batches (do not overcrowd the pot) and sear for 2-3 minutes per batch until browned on all sides. Remove the beef and set aside. If using whole spices, add them now and toast for 30 seconds until fragrant.
WHY: Searing creates a flavourful crust (Maillard reaction) that adds depth to the broth; toasting spices releases their essential oils.
Build the Curry Base

Reduce heat to medium. Add the onion quarters to the pot and cook for 2-3 minutes until lightly coloured. Return the beef to the pot. Pour in the remaining coconut milk and stock. Add fish sauce, palm sugar, and tamarind paste. Stir to dissolve the sugar and bring to a gentle simmer.
WHY: Onions provide sweetness and body; the liquid ratio creates a rich but not overly thick sauce.
Slow Simmer the Beef

Cover the pot with a lid slightly ajar. Maintain a gentle simmer for 90 minutes, or until the beef is tender and yielding. Check occasionally and skim any excess fat from the surface. The sauce should slowly thicken and deepen in colour.
WHY: Long, slow cooking breaks down the connective tissue in the chuck, creating meltingly tender beef and a rich, collagen-infused sauce.
Add Potatoes and Finish

Add the cubed potatoes to the pot. Simmer uncovered for 30 minutes, or until the potatoes are tender and the sauce has thickened nicely. Stir in the roasted peanuts (reserve some for garnish). Taste and adjust seasoning with fish sauce, sugar, or salt - it should be rich, slightly sweet, and savoury with gentle warmth.
WHY: Potatoes absorb the spiced broth and add body; peanuts provide texture and nuttiness characteristic of Massaman.
Rest and Serve

Remove from heat and let the curry rest for 5 minutes - this allows the flavours to meld and the sauce to settle. Serve over steamed jasmine rice, garnished with coriander, spring onions, sliced chillies, and reserved roasted peanuts.
WHY: Resting time allows the flavours to harmonise and the sauce to reach the perfect consistency.
Chef's Note

For the most authentic flavour, make your own Massaman paste by blending dried chillies, lemongrass, galangal, garlic, shallots, coriander seeds, cumin, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and shrimp paste. Store-bought paste works well for convenience.
WHY: Homemade paste provides the freshest, most vibrant spice profile, but quality store-bought paste delivers excellent results.
pair: Off-dry Riesling's acidity and slight sweetness balance the rich, spiced coconut sauce without overpowering the delicate aromatics.
Cool, creamy, and sweet - the perfect foil to Massaman's warm spices and rich coconut base.

blurb: Vietnam's iconic fragrant beef noodle soup with crystal-clear broth, rice noodles, and tender beef slices.

prov.popular_in: Vietnam (northern and southern regions); globally popular Vietnamese diaspora dish
prov.famous_for: Crystal-clear, deeply aromatic beef broth infused with charred aromatics and warm spices; Vietnam's national dish
prov.consists_of: Rice noodles in clear beef broth topped with various beef cuts, fresh herbs, bean sprouts, and condiments
prov.name_origin: From the French 'pot-au-feu' (beef stew), adopted during French colonial period; adapted to Vietnamese tastes with local aromatics and noodles
prov.why_loved: The depth of flavour from hours of simmering with charred aromatics, the comfort of hot noodles, and the ritual of customising each bowl with fresh herbs and condiments
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — beef bones simmered 6+ hours with charred aromatics and spices; noodles cooked separately and assembled table-side

brief.lead: Vietnam's most iconic dish - a soulful, crystal-clear beef broth simmered for hours with charred onion and ginger, cinnamon, star anise, and cardamom, served over flat rice noodles with tender beef slices, and crowned with a vibrant garden of fresh herbs, bean sprouts, and chillies that each diner adds to taste.
expect: Crystal-Clear Broth
Deeply beefy yet pristine, infused with the sweet warmth of charred aromatics and the fragrant spice blend of star anise, cinnamon, and cardamom.
Multiple Beef Textures
Rare brisket slices, well-done flank, and tender beef balls offer a range of textures and flavour concentrations.
Fresh Herb Garden
Mint, coriander, Thai basil, and bean sprouts add bright, fresh contrasts to the rich, aromatic broth.
Custom Condiment Ritual
Each diner customises their bowl with hoisin, chilli sauce, lime, and fresh herbs - part of the phở experience.

ing: large pieces of ginger (10 cm each), unpeeled
spring onions, white and green parts separated
tl: Blanch bones; char aromatics over open flame
Simmer bones with charred aromatics and spices
Strain broth; season with fish sauce and sugar
Slice beef; cook noodles; prep herbs
Compose bowls; pour broth; add toppings
steps: Prepare and Blanch Bones

Rinse the beef bones thoroughly under cold water. In a large pot, bring water to a boil. Add the bones and blanch for 5 minutes - this removes impurities and blood. Drain and rinse the bones under cold water until the water runs clear. Clean the pot.
WHY: Blanching removes blood and impurities that would cloud the broth, resulting in the crystal-clear phở broth that defines the dish.
Char the Aromatics

Using a blowtorch, gas stove, or oven broiler, char the unpeeled onions and ginger until blackened on the outside - about 10-15 minutes. This creates a sweet, smoky depth. Peel away the blackened layers (don't remove all the char).
WHY: Charring caramelises the natural sugars in the aromatics, adding the distinctive sweet-smoky depth that defines authentic phở broth.
Start the Broth

Place the blanched bones in a very large stock pot (minimum 8 litres). Add 6 litres of cold water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a very gentle simmer. Skim any scum that rises to the surface for the first 30 minutes.
WHY: Starting with cold water allows for maximum flavour extraction; constant skimming ensures a clear broth.
Add Aromatics and Spices

Add the charred onions and ginger to the pot. Create a spice bundle with the star anise, cinnamon, cardamom, clove, coriander seeds, and fennel seeds (use cheesecloth or a spice bag). Add the spice bundle to the pot. Simmer gently, uncovered, for 4-6 hours. The broth should reduce by about one-third and develop a rich, amber colour.
WHY: The spice bundle infuses the broth with warm aromatics without leaving gritty residue; long simmering extracts maximum flavour from the bones.
Season and Strain

Remove the spice bundle. Add fish sauce and rock sugar. Simmer for another 30 minutes. Taste and adjust with salt if needed. Strain the broth through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean container. Discard the solids. The broth can be refrigerated overnight and the fat skimmed from the surface for a lighter result.
WHY: Fish sauce adds umami depth; sugar balances the saltiness; straining removes all solids for the pristine clear broth.
Prepare the Components

Soak the rice noodles in hot water for 30 minutes, then drain. Slice the beef flank or sirloin as thinly as possible against the grain - partially freezing the beef for 20 minutes makes this easier. Slice the brisket into thin pieces. Prepare all herbs and condiments.
WHY: Thin slices of beef cook quickly in the hot broth; presoaking noodles ensures they cook evenly in the broth.
Assemble and Serve

Bring the strained broth to a rolling boil. Blanch the noodles for 30 seconds in boiling water, then divide among serving bowls. Arrange the sliced beef, brisket, and onions on top of the noodles. Pour the boiling broth over the beef (it should cook to medium-rare from the heat). Garnish generously with herbs, bean sprouts, spring onion greens, and white pepper. Serve immediately with lime wedges, hoisin sauce, and chilli sauce on the side.
WHY: Assembling table-side allows each diner to customise with fresh herbs and condiments - an essential part of the phở experience.
Chef's Note

For the most authentic experience, serve the broth in a small pot at the table so diners can add more hot broth to their bowl as they eat. This keeps the noodles from getting soggy and ensures every bite is piping hot.
WHY: This is how phở is traditionally enjoyed in Vietnam, keeping the broth hot and the noodles perfectly textured throughout the meal.
pair: Crisp, cold lager cuts through the rich broth and refreshes the palate between the aromatic, spicy sips.
Vietnamese Iced Coffee (Cà Phê Sữa Đá)
The perfect start - strong, sweet coffee contrasts beautifully with phở's savoury warmth, bridging to the main course.

blurb: Iconic Greek casserole with layers of eggplant, rich beef mince, and creamy béchamel topping.

prov.popular_in: Greece; throughout the Mediterranean and Greek diaspora communities
prov.famous_for: Layers of fried eggplant and beef mince topped with a creamy béchamel sauce; Greece's most beloved comfort food
prov.consists_of: Layered casserole with fried eggplant, tomatoey beef mince sauce, and a creamy béchamel custard topping, baked until golden
prov.name_origin: From Arabic 'musaqqa'a' meaning 'chilled' or 'to moisten'; the modern Greek version was created in the 1920s by chef Nikolaos Tselementes
prov.why_loved: The ultimate comfort food - rich, savoury, and satisfying with layers of contrasting textures from silky eggplant to hearty meat sauce to creamy topping
prov.method_detail: Oven — eggplant fried, layered with meat sauce, topped with béchamel, and baked until golden and bubbling

brief.lead: Greece's iconic layered casserole featuring silky fried eggplant, a rich tomatoey beef mince sauce fragrant with cinnamon and allspice, all crowned with a luxurious béchamel custard topping that bakes to golden perfection - a dish of hearty comfort and sophisticated flavours that defines Greek home cooking.
expect: Silky Fried Eggplant
Fried until meltingly tender, the eggplant layers provide richness and a silky texture that contrasts beautifully with the meat sauce.
Fragrant Meat Sauce
Beef mince simmered with tomatoes, onions, and warm spices like cinnamon and allspice - the savoury heart of the dish.
Creamy Béchamel Topping
The luxurious crown - a creamy white sauce enriched with egg and cheese that bakes to a golden, velvety perfection.
Hearty Comfort
Substantial, satisfying, and deeply savoury - the ultimate comfort food that's perfect for feeding a crowd.

ing: flaky sea salt and black pepper
tl: Slice and salt eggplant; fry until golden
Sauté vegetables and beef; add tomatoes and spices
Simmer meat sauce 30 min until thick
Prepare white sauce; enrich with eggs and cheese
Bake 45-60 min until golden and bubbling
steps: Prepare the Eggplant

Slice the eggplants lengthwise into 1 cm thick slices. Sprinkle generously with salt and set aside for 30 minutes - this draws out bitterness and excess moisture. Pat the slices thoroughly dry with paper towels.
WHY: Salting eggplant removes bitter compounds and prevents the slices from absorbing too much oil during frying.
Fry the Eggplant

Heat oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat (180°C). Fry the eggplant slices in batches until golden on both sides - about 2-3 minutes per side. Drain on paper towels and season lightly with salt.
WHY: Frying creates a tender, silky texture and prevents the eggplant from becoming mushy during baking.
Start the Meat Sauce

In the same pan, heat 2 tablespoons of olive oil over medium heat. Add the chopped onions and cook for 8-10 minutes until softened and translucent. Add the minced garlic and cook for 1 minute until fragrant. Add the beef mince and cook, breaking it up, until browned all over.
WHY: Building the sauce base properly creates depth of flavour; browning the beef adds savoury complexity.
Simmer the Sauce

Add the chopped tomatoes, tomato purée, red wine (if using), cinnamon, allspice, oregano, salt, and pepper. Stir well and simmer gently for 30 minutes, or until the sauce has thickened nicely. Taste and adjust seasoning - it should be rich and savoury with gentle warmth from the spices.
WHY: Simmering develops the flavours and allows the sauce to thicken for better layering.
Make the Béchamel

In a medium saucepan, melt the butter over medium heat. Add the flour and whisk for 1-2 minutes to cook out the raw flour taste. Gradually whisk in the warm milk, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. Simmer gently for 5-10 minutes until thickened. Remove from heat and whisk in the beaten eggs and grated cheese. Season with salt and white pepper.
WHY: Cooking the flour (roux) prevents a raw taste; adding eggs off-heat prevents scrambling; cheese adds savoury depth.
Assemble the Moussaka

Preheat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan-assisted). Arrange a layer of eggplant slices in the bottom of a greased baking dish (30 x 20 cm). Spread half the meat sauce over the eggplant. Repeat with another layer of eggplant and the remaining meat sauce. Finish with a final layer of eggplant. Pour the béchamel evenly over the top. Sprinkle with parmesan and oregano.
WHY: Proper layering ensures every serving gets all components; the béchamel topping creates the signature golden crown.
Bake and Rest

Bake for 45-60 minutes, or until the béchamel is golden and bubbling, and the sides are pulling away from the dish. Remove from oven and let rest for 15-20 minutes before serving - this is crucial for clean slices.
WHY: Resting allows the layers to set so the moussaka holds its shape when sliced.
Chef's Note

For the most authentic flavour, don't omit the cinnamon and allspice - these warm spices are what distinguish Greek moussaka from other layered casseroles. Some versions also include a layer of sliced potatoes; par-boil them first before layering.
WHY: The warm spices echo the dish's Ottoman past and create the distinctive Greek flavour profile.
pair: Crisp, mineral-driven Greek white with high acidity cuts through the rich béchamel and eggplant while complementing the spiced meat sauce.
Cool, crisp, and refreshing - the perfect balance to moussaka's richness with tangy feta, fresh vegetables, and olive oil.

blurb: West Africa's iconic party rice - smoky, savoury, and infused with tomatoes, peppers, and aromatic spices.

prov.popular_in: Nigeria, Ghana, Senegal, Gambia, and throughout West Africa; the subject of friendly 'Jollof Wars' between countries
prov.famous_for: The beloved 'party rice' of West Africa; smoky, flavourful one-pot rice dish that's the centerpiece of celebrations and gatherings
prov.consists_of: Long-grain rice cooked in a rich tomato, pepper, and onion stew with aromatic spices, creating distinctively flavourful grains
prov.name_origin: From the Wolof people and Jolof Empire (14th-16th centuries) in present-day Senegal/Gambia; the dish spread from Saint Louis, Senegal across West Africa
prov.why_loved: The perfect party dish - smoky, savoury, and deeply flavourful rice that feeds a crowd and brings people together; each region claims their version is best
prov.method_detail: Stovetop one-pot — rice cooked in tomato-pepper base with 'party jollof' smoky flavour from burnt tomato residue

brief.lead: West Africa's most celebrated dish - a fragrant, smoky one-pot rice where long-grain grains are cooked to perfection in a rich tomato, pepper, and onion stew infused with curry, thyme, and bay leaves, creating the signature 'party jollof' flavour that's the centerpiece of celebrations across Nigeria, Ghana, and beyond.
expect: Smoky Party Jollof Flavour
The coveted smoky depth that comes from the 'burnt tomato' technique - the essence of authentic party jollof.
Flavourful Grains
Each grain absorbs the tomato-pepper base and spices, creating rice that's fragrant and savoury in every bite.
Versatile Crowd-Pleaser
Perfect for feeding a crowd - equally beloved for solo dinners, date nights, and large celebrations.
Regional Variations
Nigerian (smokier, more intense), Ghanaian (more tomato-forward), Senegalese (includes fish) - each region proudly claims superiority.

ing: red onions (1 for base, 1 for garnish)
Scotch bonnet or habanero peppers (adjust to heat preference)
grilled chicken, beef, or fried plantains (optional)
tl: Blend tomatoes, peppers, onions for smooth base
Fry blended base until oil separates and slightly burns
Add spices, stock, rice; stir to combine
Cover and steam rice 25 min on low heat
Rest 10 min; fluff rice; garnish and serve
steps: Prepare the Jollof Base

Blend the tomatoes, peppers (remove seeds for less heat), and 1 onion until smooth. Set aside. Rinse the rice thoroughly until the water runs clear - this removes excess starch for separate grains. Soak the rice in hot water for 10 minutes, then drain.
WHY: A smooth blended base creates the silky texture that coats each grain evenly; rinsing rice prevents gummy results.
Create the Party Jollof Smoky Base

In a large heavy-bottomed pot, heat the oil over medium heat. Add the blended tomato-pepper base and tomato purée. Fry for 15-20 minutes, stirring frequently, until the mixture has reduced significantly, the oil has separated, and you see slight browning/caramelisation at the edges - this 'burnt' effect creates the signature smoky party jollof flavour.
WHY: This reduction and caramelisation is the secret to the coveted smoky depth that distinguishes authentic jollof from plain rice.
Add Spices and Build Flavour

Add the curry powder, thyme, bay leaves, grated ginger, garlic, and crumbled stock cube (if using). Stir for 1 minute until fragrant. Add the remaining chopped onion and cook for 2 minutes until softened.
WHY: Blooming the spices in the hot base releases their aromatic compounds and creates the complex flavour profile.
Add Rice and Liquid

Add the drained rice to the pot. Stir gently to coat every grain with the flavoured base - this is crucial for even colouring. Pour in the stock. Taste and add salt as needed. The liquid should be about 2 cm above the rice level.
WHY: Coating the rice ensures every grain absorbs the flavours; proper liquid ratio prevents mushy or undercooked rice.
Steam the Rice

Increase the heat to high and bring to a boil. Once boiling, reduce heat to the lowest setting, cover tightly with a lid, and steam for 25 minutes. Do not lift the lid - trapping the steam ensures even cooking.
WHY: The steaming method allows the rice to absorb the flavoured liquid gradually, creating separate, flavourful grains.
Rest and Fluff

Remove from heat and let rest, covered, for 10 minutes. This allows the moisture to redistribute evenly. Uncover and fluff gently with a fork. Taste and adjust salt if needed. Garnish with spring onions, coriander/parsley, and fresh tomato slices.
WHY: Resting time ensures even moisture distribution; gentle fluffing separates the grains without breaking them.
Serve and Celebrate

Jollof rice is traditionally served as the centerpiece at parties and celebrations. Accompany with grilled chicken, beef, fried plantains, or a simple salad. The 'Jollof Wars' between Nigeria and Ghana are legendary - each country claims their version is superior. Try both and decide your favourite!
WHY: Jollof is more than a dish - it's a cultural icon that brings people together and sparks friendly rivalries across West Africa.
Chef's Note

For the most authentic party jollof flavour, some cooks deliberately let the tomato base catch slightly at the bottom of the pot (being careful not to burn it). This 'burnt tomato' technique is what creates the distinctive smoky flavour. Another trick: add a piece of smoked fish or beef to the pot for extra depth.
WHY: These techniques are the secrets that distinguish home-cooked jollof from restaurant versions and create the beloved party flavour.
pair: Cold, crisp Nigerian lager perfectly balances jollof's rich, smoky flavours and refreshes the palate at celebrations.
The classic starter - spicy, smoky beef skewers with groundnut spice coating that pairs perfectly with jollof rice.

blurb: Sichuan's iconic numbing-spicy tofu dish with silky tofu in a fiery, aromatic chilli bean sauce.

prov.popular_in: Sichuan Province, China; globally popular as a symbol of Sichuan cuisine
prov.famous_for: The 'mala' (numbing-spicy) flavour profile from Sichuan peppercorns and chillies; China's fiery global ambassador
prov.consists_of: Silken tofu and minced pork/beef in a rich sauce flavoured with doubanjiang (broad bean chilli paste) and Sichuan peppercorns
prov.name_origin: Named after 'Mapo' ('pockmarked old woman'), Chen Senfu, who created the dish at her Chengdu restaurant in the 1860s-1870s Qing Dynasty
prov.why_loved: The addictive interplay of silky tofu, fiery heat, and numbing Sichuan peppercorns creates an unforgettable flavour experience
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — doubanjiang and aromatics bloomed in oil, then tofu braised in chilli-bean sauce until infused

brief.lead: Sichuan's most famous export - a fiery, numbing bowl of silken tofu swimming in a rich, aromatic sauce flavoured with the holy trinity of Sichuan cooking: doubanjiang (fermented broad bean chilli paste) for depth, Sichuan peppercorns for signature numbing heat, and fresh chillies for fire - a dish that perfectly embodies the 'mala' (numbing-spicy) sensation that defines Sichuan cuisine.
expect: Signature 'Mala' Sensation
The addictive combination of Sichuan peppercorn's numbing buzz and chilli's fiery heat - the essence of Sichuan cuisine.
Silky Tofu Texture
Tender silken tofu that absorbs the rich sauce while maintaining its delicate, custard-like texture.
Complex Umami Depth
Doubanjiang (fermented broad bean paste) provides deep, fermented umami that can't be replicated with fresh ingredients alone.
Comforting Heat
A warming, comforting dish that's both fiery and soothing - perfect for cold weather or when you need a flavour wake-up.

ing: spring onion, white and green parts separated
tl: Cut tofu into cubes; set aside
Fry doubanjiang, garlic, ginger until fragrant
Add mince; brown and break up
Add liquid and tofu; simmer gently
Add cornflour slurry; season with soy sauce
Garnish with spring onions, coriander, peppercorns
steps: Prepare the Tofu
Cut the silken tofu into 2 cm cubes. Gently place the cubes in a bowl of warm, salted water for 5 minutes - this firms the tofu slightly and removes excess moisture. Drain carefully and set aside.
WHY: Salting water firms the tofu so it holds its shape during cooking while remaining silky inside.
Toast Sichuan Peppercorns
In a dry pan over medium heat, toast the Sichuan peppercorns for 1-2 minutes until fragrant (be careful not to burn). Grind to a fine powder. Set aside.
WHY: Toasting releases the citrusy, numbing aromatic compounds in the peppercorns, intensifying the flavour.
Bloom the Aromatics

Heat the oil in a wok or deep frying pan over medium heat. Add the doubanjiang and fry for 1-2 minutes, stirring constantly, until the oil turns red and the paste is fragrant - this 'blooming' releases the paste's flavours. Add the garlic, ginger, and spring onion whites. Fry for 30 seconds until fragrant.
WHY: Blooming the doubanjiang is crucial - it transforms the paste into a rich, aromatic base for the dish.
Brown the Meat
Add the minced meat to the wok. Break it up and fry for 3-4 minutes until browned and cooked through. If using dried whole chillies, add them now.
WHY: Browning the meat adds flavour and texture; the rendered fat carries the aromatic flavours through the dish.
Add Stock and Simmer

Pour in the chicken stock or water. Add the Shaoxing wine if using. Bring to a gentle simmer. Gently slide the tofu cubes into the sauce - try not to break them. Simmer for 5 minutes, very gently stirring once or twice to coat the tofu in the sauce.
WHY: Gentle simmering allows the tofu to absorb the flavours while maintaining its delicate texture.
Thicken and Season

Give the cornflour slurry a stir and add it to the wok. Gently stir for 30 seconds until the sauce thickens slightly and coats the back of a spoon. Add the soy sauce and half the ground Sichuan peppercorns. Taste and adjust with salt if needed.
WHY: The slight thickening creates a velvety sauce that clings to the tofu; soy sauce adds umami depth.
Finish and Serve

Remove from heat. Transfer to a serving bowl. Garnish generously with spring onion greens, coriander, and the remaining ground Sichuan peppercorns. Drizzle with chilli oil if desired. Serve immediately with steamed jasmine rice.
WHY: Garnishing at the end preserves the fresh aromatics and vibrant colour; the finishing Sichuan pepper kick intensifies the numbing sensation.
Chef's Note

For the most authentic flavour, use Pixian doubanjiang from Sichuan - it has a depth of flavour that can't be matched by other brands. The ratio of tofu to meat in authentic mapo tofu is about 10:1 - the meat is there for flavour, not bulk. This dish should be fiery and numbing - adjust the doubanjiang and Sichuan pepper to your heat tolerance.
WHY: Quality doubanjiang and proper ratios are what distinguish restaurant-quality mapo tofu from home versions.
pair: Cold, crisp lager provides refreshing relief from mapo tofu's fiery heat while complementing the savoury flavours.
A bowl of hot and sour soup awakens the palate with its contrasting tangy heat, preparing it for mapo's numbing fire.

blurb: Thailand's beloved spicy-sour-sweet salad of shredded green papaya pounded with chillies, garlic, lime, and fish sauce.

prov.popular_in: Thailand (especially Isan/Northeastern region); also Laos where it's called 'tam mak hoong'
prov.famous_for: Thailand's national salad - the perfect balance of sour, sweet, salty, and spicy flavours; beloved street food and everyday staple
prov.consists_of: Shredded unripe green papaya pounded with garlic, chillies, fish sauce, lime juice, palm sugar, and topped with peanuts, dried shrimp, and tomatoes
prov.name_origin: From 'som' (sour) and 'tum' (pounded) in Thai/Isan; the dish originated in Laos/Isan and spread throughout Thailand
prov.why_loved: The addictive interplay of textures and flavours - crunchy papaya, salty-sour-sweet-savoury dressing, and the heat of fresh chillies creates a perfectly balanced, refreshing dish
prov.method_detail: Mortar and pestle pounding — aromatics and dressing ingredients pounded together, then papaya added and gently pounded to absorb flavours

brief.lead: The quintessential taste of Isan - a vibrant, refreshing salad where shredded unripe green papaya is gently pounded in a mortar with garlic, bird's eye chillies, fish sauce, lime juice, and palm sugar until it absorbs the perfect balance of sour, sweet, salty, and spicy, then topped with roasted peanuts, dried shrimp, crisp beans, and juicy tomatoes for a dish that's at once fiery, refreshing, and impossible to stop eating.
expect: Perfect Four-Balance
The harmonious interplay of sour (lime), sweet (palm sugar), salty (fish sauce), and spicy (fresh chillies) - the essence of Thai cuisine.
Crunchy Papaya Texture
Shredded green papaya provides a crisp, fresh crunch that's the perfect canvas for the bold dressing.
Pounded Infusion
The mortar and pestle technique forces the flavours into the papaya, creating deeper flavour integration than tossing alone.
Customisable Heat
Adjust the chillies to your preference - from gentle warmth to fiery Isan-style heat that makes you sweat.

ing: bird's eye chillies (adjust to heat preference)
long beans or green beans, cut into 5 cm pieces
fermented fish sauce (pla ra) - for authentic Isan flavour
tl: Peel and shred papaya; set aside
Pound garlic, chillies, sugar, fish sauce, lime
Add papaya and beans; pound gently
Gently fold in tomatoes, peanuts, dried shrimp
Adjust seasoning; serve with sticky rice
steps: Prepare the Green Papaya

Peel the green papaya. Using a julienne peeler, sharp knife, or traditional Thai shredder, shred the papaya into long, thin strips. Place in a large bowl of cold water with a pinch of salt for 5 minutes - this crisps the papaya. Drain thoroughly.
WHY: Soaking in salted water firms and crisps the papaya, creating the signature crunch.
Pound the Aromatics

In a large granite mortar, add the garlic and bird's eye chillies. Pound firmly with the pestle for 30 seconds until you have a coarse paste - you want some chilli pieces remaining for texture, not a smooth purée.
WHY: Pounding releases the aromatic oils and creates a paste that distributes flavour evenly.
Build the Dressing

Add the palm sugar (or tamarind paste), fish sauce, and lime juice to the mortar. Pound for another 30 seconds until the sugar dissolves and the ingredients are well combined. Taste - adjust with more fish sauce, lime, or sugar to achieve the perfect balance of salty, sour, sweet, and spicy.
WHY: Building the dressing in the mortar allows the flavours to meld and the sugar to dissolve properly.
Add the Papaya and Beans

Add the shredded papaya and long beans to the mortar. Using a large spoon or the pestle, gently pound and toss for 1-2 minutes. You're not trying to mash the papaya - you're gently bruising it to help it absorb the dressing while maintaining its crunch.
WHY: Gentle pounding forces the flavours into the papaya without destroying the texture - this is the secret to authentic som tum.
Fold in the Toppings

Add the tomatoes and peanuts (and dried shrimp if using). Gently fold everything together with a large spoon - do not pound at this stage. Taste and adjust seasoning if needed. The salad should be bright, balanced, and punchy.
WHY: Folding preserves the texture of tomatoes and peanuts while evenly distributing them.
Serve Immediately
Transfer to a serving plate or bowl. Serve immediately with sticky rice and lime wedges on the side. Som tum is best enjoyed fresh - the flavours are brightest and the crunch is at its peak.
WHY: Som tum doesn't keep well - the papaya loses crunch and the dressing dilutes. It's meant to be eaten immediately.
Chef's Note

For the most authentic Isan flavour, add a small amount of fermented fish sauce (pla ra) - it adds a funky depth that's characteristic of the region's cuisine. The traditional way to eat som tum is with sticky rice (khao neeo) - use your hands to form small balls of sticky rice and dip them into the dressing as you eat the salad.
WHY: Pla ra is the secret ingredient that distinguishes Isan-style som tum from Central Thai versions; sticky rice is the traditional accompaniment.
pair: The essential accompaniment - clumps of sticky rice are dipped into the dressing, balancing the heat and providing a satisfying, neutral canvas.
Cold, crisp lager provides refreshing relief from som tum's fiery chillies while complementing the salty-sour flavours.

blurb: Korea's most famous export - tender, sweet-savoury marinated beef grilled to smoky perfection.

prov.popular_in: Korea; globally popular as Korea's representative dish
prov.famous_for: Korea's most celebrated dish - thinly sliced beef marinated in sweet-savoury sauce and grilled to tender perfection
prov.consists_of: Thinly sliced beef (typically ribeye or sirloin) marinated in soy sauce, sugar, Asian pear, garlic, sesame oil, then grilled
prov.name_origin: From 'bul' (fire) and 'gogi' (meat) in Korean; evolved from 'maekjeok' (skewered meat) from the Goguryeo Dynasty (37 BCE-668 CE)
prov.why_loved: The perfect balance of sweet, savoury, and smoky flavours with impossibly tender meat that melts in your mouth
prov.method_detail: Grill or pan-fry — marinated beef quickly grilled over high heat or pan-seared until caramelised and slightly charred

brief.lead: Korea's beloved barbecue dish - paper-thin slices of premium beef, traditionally ribeye, marinated for hours in a sweet-savoury blend of soy sauce, Asian pear (the secret tenderiser), garlic, sesame oil, and aromatics, then grilled over high heat until caramelised with gorgeous char-grilled edges, resulting in meat that's impossibly tender, juicy, and packed with the flavours that have made Korean BBQ famous worldwide.
expect: Melt-in-Your-Mouth Tenderness
Thinly sliced beef and the enzymatic tenderising from Asian pear create meat that's impossibly tender and juicy.
Sweet-Savoury Marinade
The perfect balance of soy sauce's savouriness, fruit's sweetness, garlic's pungency, and sesame oil's nuttiness.
Caramelised Char
High-heat grilling creates gorgeous char-grilled edges with caramelised flavour - the essence of Korean BBQ.
Interactive Dining
Grill table-side (if possible) and wrap in lettuce with ssamjang, garlic, and kimchi - the full Korean BBQ experience.
kit: Grill (gas or charcoal) or heavy frying pan

ing: beef ribeye or sirloin, sliced paper-thin (partially freeze for easier slicing)
red leaf lettuce or perilla leaves
tl: Combine beef with marinade; refrigerate 2 hours minimum
Heat grill or pan to high; oil grates
Cook beef 1-2 min per side until charred
Rest 5 min; serve with lettuce wraps
steps: Prepare the Beef

Partially freeze the beef for 20-30 minutes - this makes it easier to slice thinly. Slice the beef against the grain into paper-thin slices (2-3 mm). If you can't slice thinly enough, ask your butcher to slice it for you.
WHY: Thin slices cook quickly and evenly, creating the tender texture that defines bulgogi.
Make the Marinade

In a bowl or zip-lock bag, combine the grated Asian pear, minced garlic, grated onion, soy sauce, sugar or honey, sesame oil, rice vinegar (if using), and black pepper. Mix well until the sugar dissolves.
WHY: The Asian pear contains enzymes that tenderise the meat while adding natural sweetness - it's the secret to authentic bulgogi.
Marinate the Beef

Add the beef slices to the marinade. Massage gently to coat every piece. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, preferably overnight - the longer the marinating time, the more tender and flavourful the beef. For best results, turn the meat halfway through.
WHY: Long marinating allows the flavours to penetrate deeply and the pear enzymes to work their magic on the meat.
Prepare the Grill

Heat a grill (gas or charcoal) or a heavy cast-iron frying pan to high heat. Lightly oil the grates or pan. The cooking surface should be very hot - this creates the signature char-grilled flavour and caramelisation.
WHY: High heat is essential for quick cooking that develops char-grilled flavour without overcooking the meat.
Grill the Bulgogi

Remove the beef from the marinade, letting excess drip off (don't pat dry - the marinade creates caramelisation). Grill the beef in batches for 1-2 minutes per side, or until nicely charred and cooked through. Don't overcrowd the grill - this causes steaming instead of grilling.
WHY: Quick high-heat cooking creates the char-grilled edges while keeping the interior juicy and tender.
Rest and Serve

Let the beef rest for 5 minutes on a platter. This allows the juices to redistribute. Sprinkle with sesame seeds and spring onion. Serve immediately with lettuce leaves, ssamjang, kimchi, grilled garlic, and green chillies for wrapping.
WHY: Resting ensures juicy meat; wrapping in lettuce with condiments is the traditional Korean way to enjoy bulgogi.
Korean BBQ Style Serving

To eat, take a lettuce leaf, add a piece of bulgogi, a small amount of ssamjang, a slice of garlic, some kimchi, and a piece of green chilli. Wrap it up and enjoy in one bite. This combination of textures and flavours is the authentic Korean BBQ experience.
WHY: The lettuce wrap provides freshness and crunch that balances the rich, savoury meat - this is how bulgogi is traditionally enjoyed.
Chef's Note

For the most authentic flavour, don't omit the Asian pear - it's the key tenderiser and flavour enhancer. If you can't find Asian pear, a very firm Bosc pear or a Granny Smith apple works as a substitute. The traditional way to slice the beef is to use a sharp knife while the meat is partially frozen - this creates the paper-thin slices that are essential for proper bulgogi.
WHY: Proper slicing technique and the right fruit are what distinguish restaurant-quality bulgogi from home versions.
pair: The traditional accompaniment to Korean BBQ - clean, neutral spirits that cleanse the palate between rich, savoury bites of bulgogi.
Crispy, savoury kimchi pancakes with their tangy heat make the perfect starter to whet the appetite for bulgogi's sweet-savoury richness.
